perturbation.original_text: 3.5. Due Date for Payment. Your Royalty payments and Advertising Contributions are due by the first (1st) day after the end of each Period (or the next business day if the first day is a Sunday or federal holiday) (as of the Agreement Date, the due date is Monday of each week). You must pay all other amounts due to us as specified in this Agreement or, if no time is specified, such amounts are due upon receipt of an invoice from us.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.5. Due Date for Payment. Your Royalty payments and Advertising Contributions are due by the fifteenth (15th) day after the end of each Period. You must pay all other amounts due to us as specified in this Agreement or, if no time is specified, such amounts are due 60 days from the date of invoice.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the payment terms for royalty and advertising fees to the 15th day after the period and other invoices to 60 days introduces non-compliance. It violates federal wage laws and Florida state laws that mandate more prompt payment.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Fair Labor Standards Act (FLSA) and Florida Statutes § 532.01
perturbation.law_explanation: The FLSA mandates prompt and regular payment of wages. Florida Statute § 532.01 governs payment of wages and prohibits unreasonable delays. The changed text could create delays not compliant with wage payment laws.
perturbation.justification: The changed text alters payment due dates, potentially conflicting with wage laws that mandate prompt payment, as highlighted by the Law Explanation. While GovInfo alone is insufficient to confirm a specific violation, the Perturbation Explanation states the modification violates federal wage laws and Florida state laws, and the Law Explanation mentions the FLSA's prompt payment mandate. The original text specified payment shortly after the end of each period, whereas the changed text extends this to 15 days after the period for royalties and advertising, and 60 days from invoice for other amounts, creating a contradiction to the prompt payment laws.

perturbation.original_text: 4.1.2 You must submit a site review report and such other information or materials as we may reasonably require (including but not limited to, photographs, demographic information, an option contract, letter of intent, or other evidence satisfactory to us that confirms your favorable prospects for obtaining the site). We will review site approval submissions on a first-in basis. (a) If we do not approve in writing the proposed site, you must, within 30 days after our disapproval of the proposed site, submit a new proposed site within the Delivery/Catering and Advertising Area for our review and approval. (b) By no later than 90 days after the Agreement Date, you must have obtained our written approval of a site. If you have not done so, we may elect to terminate this Agreement, and we will not refund to you the Initial Franchise Fee or any other money paid to us. (c) We will not unreasonably withhold approval of any site that meets our standards. You may not lease or otherwise acquire the right to occupy the proposed site without our prior written approval.
perturbation.changed_text: 4.1.2 You must submit a site review report and such other information or materials as we may reasonably require. We will review site approval submissions. (a) If we do not approve the proposed site, you must, within 5 days after our disapproval, submit a new proposed site. (b) By no later than 30 days after the Agreement Date, you must obtain our written approval of a site. If you have not done so, we may terminate this Agreement, and we will not refund any money paid to us. (c) We may withhold approval of any site for any reason.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the time for submitting a new site and getting approval, and allowing the franchisor to withhold approval for any reason is an issue. This can violate good faith principles and fair dealing, depending on the jurisdiction and specific laws that protect franchisees from arbitrary or unfair practices by the franchisor.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Implied Covenant of Good Faith and Fair Dealing
perturbation.law_explanation: Contracts have an implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. Shortening the deadlines and allowing arbitrary disapproval of sites could violate this covenant, making it an unfair or bad-faith practice.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: good faith | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you good faith Good faith is a broad term that’s used to encompass honest dealing. Depending on the exact setting, good faith may require an honest belief or purpose, faithful performance of duties , observance of fair dealing standards, or an absence of fraudulent intent . A fiduciary relationship creates a duty of good faith between the agent and the principal . The breach of this duty of good faith can lead to liability . Failure to act in good faith is known as bad faith and is generally considered to be a level of culpability greater than negligence . [Last reviewed in January of 2023 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE business law contracts wex definitions business sectors legal education and practice Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The modified text contradicts the implied covenant of good faith and fair dealing. The original text included reasonableness standards for site approval, protecting the franchisee. The changed text allows the franchisor to withhold approval 'for any reason,' removing this protection and potentially violating good faith, making it an unfair practice. Scraped Law Snippet 1 defines good faith as encompassing 'honest dealing' and requiring 'observance of fair dealing standards,' which the altered contract arguably violates by allowing arbitrary denial. The original text gives franchisee 30 days to submit a new proposal after disapproval, while the modified version only gives 5 days. Original text provides 90 days to obtain written approval while the modified version only provides 30 days. Therefore the changed version creates a significant contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: 15.3. Termination by Us Following Expiration of Cure Period for Monetary Default. You will be in default under this Agreement if you fail, refuse, or neglect to pay when due (including if we are not able to collect payments by electronic fund transfer pursuant to Section 3.6 [Method of Payment] due to insufficient funds in your account(s), closure of your account(s), or any other reason resulting in the nonpayment) any monies owing to us, our affiliates, or any lender that has provided financing to you under this Agreement or any other agreement, or to your landlord and/or any supplier of goods or services to your Franchised Business. You will have ten (10) days after written notice of such default from us within which to remedy the default. You may avoid termination by curing the default to our satisfaction within the 10-day period (or such longer period as applicable law may require). If you do not cure the default within such 10-day period (or such longer period as applicable law may require), this Agreement will terminate automatically and without further notice, effective immediately upon the expiration of the specified time period.
perturbation.changed_text: 15.3. Termination by Us Following Expiration of Cure Period for Monetary Default. You will be in default under this Agreement if you fail, refuse, or neglect to pay when due any monies owing to us, our affiliates, or any lender that has provided financing to you. You will have two (2) days after written notice of such default from us within which to remedy the default. If you do not cure the default within such 2-day period, this Agreement will terminate automatically and without further notice, effective immediately upon the expiration of the specified time period.
perturbation.explanation: Reducing the cure period for monetary default to two days creates a conflict. Many state franchise laws require a reasonable opportunity to cure defaults, often longer than two days, especially for significant financial obligations.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Business and Professions Code § 20020 requires a reasonable time to cure a default before termination. Two days is insufficient for correcting monetary defaults, particularly if they involve large sums or banking errors, violating California law if the franchise operates there.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, BPC 20020 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> Code Search >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Business and Professions Code - BPC DIVISION 8. SPECIAL BUSINESS REGULATIONS [18400 - 22949.92.2] ( Division 8 added by Stats. 1941, Ch. 44. ) CHAPTER 5.5. Franchise Relations [20000 - 20044] ( Chapter 5.5 added by Stats. 1980, Ch. 1355, Sec. 1. ) ARTICLE 3. Termination [20020 - 20022] ( Article 3 added by Stats. 1980, Ch. 1355, Sec. 1. ) 20020. Except as otherwise provided by this chapter, no franchisor may terminate a franchise prior to the expiration of its term, except for good cause. Except as provided in Section 20021, good cause shall be limited to the failure of the franchisee to substantially comply with the lawful requirements imposed upon the franchisee by the franchise agreement after being given notice at least 60 days in advance of the termination and a reasonable opportunity, which in no event shall be less than 60 days from the date of the notice of noncompliance, to cure the failure. The period to exercise the right to cure shall not exceed 75 days unless there is a separate agreement between the franchisor and franchisee to extend the time. (Amended by Stats. 2015, Ch. 776, Sec. 1. (AB 525) Effective January 1, 2016.)
perturbation.justification: The modified text reduces the cure period for monetary default from ten days to two days. The Perturbation Explanation states that many state franchise laws require a reasonable opportunity to cure defaults, often longer than two days. The Law Explanation specifically mentions California Business and Professions Code § 20020, which requires a reasonable time to cure a default before termination. Scraped Law Snippet 1, taken from California Code, BPC 20020, states that a franchisee must be given notice at least 60 days in advance of termination and a reasonable opportunity, which shall not be less than 60 days, to cure the failure. It also specifies that the cure period shall not exceed 75 days. The changed text providing only two days to cure a default, directly contradicts this statute. Therefore, a legal contradiction exists.
